retrieved_docs.title: Imiquimod -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Imiquimod is an immune response modifier of the imidazoquinolinamines drug class that induces the production of various cytokines, such as Interferon-alpha, Interferon-gamma, tumor necrosis factor-alpha, interleukin-1, interleukin-6, and interleukin-8. [10] [11] Imiquimod activates the innate and adaptive immune responses via binding to Toll-like receptor 7 and activation of nuclear factor kappa-B with the resultant cytokine release and recruitment of plasmacytoid predendritic cells to the site of medication application with subsequent release of Interferon-alpha and a Th1 response. [12] [13] [14] [15] In addition to its anti-viral activity, imiquimod induces apoptosis of skin cancer cells and has demonstrated anti-tumoral activity. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Human Papilloma Virus Vaccine -- Adverse Effects -- Drug-Vaccine Interactions
retrieved_docs.content: Immunosuppressive therapies, such as irradiation, alkylating agents, antimetabolites, cytotoxic medications, and corticosteroids, attenuate immune responses to the HPV vaccine.

retrieved_docs.title: Imiquimod -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the indications of imiquimod. Identify the adverse effects of imiquimod. Review the mechanism of action of imiquimod. Summarize the importance of improving care coordination amongst the interprofessional team to improve outcomes for patients receiving imiquimod. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Cisplatin -- Adverse Effects -- Drug Interactions
retrieved_docs.content: Caution is necessary when administering cisplatin with any of the following medications. Most drug interactions result in cumulative and dose-dependent hematologic, renal, and neurotoxic effects. Due to cumulative myelosuppression, cisplatin should also not be used concurrently with immunosuppressive medications. [28] [29] Alpha-lipoic acid Aminoglycosides Baricitinib BCG Chloramphenicol Clozapine Deferiprone Denosumab Dipyrone Echinacea Fosphenytoin Leflunomide Lenogristim Lipefilgrastim Natalizumab Nivolumab Ocrelizumab Palifermin Pidotimod Pimecrolimus Promazine Roflumilast Siponimod Sipuleleucel-T Tacrolimus Taxane derivatives Tertomotide Topotecan Trastuzumab Vaccinations Vinorelbine

retrieved_docs.title: Subacute Cutaneous Lupus Erythematosus  -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Tobacco is phototoxic and enhances the responsiveness of toll-like receptor 9, leading to the increased production of type 1 interferons in plasmacytoid dendritic cells and the upregulation of the expression of metalloproteinases 1 through 8. [18] Hence, smoking cessation education is very important.

retrieved_docs.title: Herpes Simplex Keratitis -- Pathophysiology -- Toll-like receptors
retrieved_docs.content: Toll-like receptors (TLRs) represent a fundamental aspect of the innate immune system, characterized by their ubiquitous presence and capacity to detect specific pathogen-associated molecular patterns (PAMPs). To this end, 13 TLRs have been identified, with 9 common to humans and mice. [44] Each TLR is tailored to recognize distinct PAMPs or ligands. In the context of HSV-1, TLRs 2, 3, and 9 play pivotal roles in identifying viral components; TLR2 interacts with viral glycoproteins at the cellular membrane, whereas TLR3 and TLR9, located within endosomes, are responsive to double-stranded RNA and unmethylated CpG motifs, respectively. The engagement of these TLRs with their respective PAMPs initiates a series of intracellular signaling pathways that culminate in cytokine production, inflammation, and the activation of an innate immune defense. [45] While the activation of TLRs constitutes a critical early mechanism for viral detection and initiation of immune responses, excessive TLR-mediated inflammation can lead to detrimental outcomes, including increased infiltration of the cornea, resulting in opacity and potential vision loss. These outcomes highlight the importance of a balanced immune response, where sufficient viral clearance is achieved without provoking adverse inflammatory reactions that compromise ocular health. [46]

retrieved_docs.title: Chromoblastomycosis -- Treatment / Management -- Moderate-to-Severe Disease
retrieved_docs.content: Imiquimod: Imiquimod is a toll-like receptor 7 agonist found to significantly improve the lesions of chromoblastomycosis when used with itraconazole with or without terbinafine. [87] [88]

retrieved_docs.title: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Both innate and adaptive immune systems play a role in the pathogenesis of SLE. The innate immune system activation is either Toll-like receptor (TLR) dependent or independent. The cell membrane-bound TLRs (TLR 2, 4, 6) are activated on exposure to the extracellular DNA and RNA from dying cells, which leads to downstream activation of the interferon regulatory family (IRF-3), NF-κB, and MAP-kinases, which serve as transcription factors for the production of proinflammatory mediators such as IFN-b. The endosomal TLRs (TLR 7, 9) are activated by single-stranded RNA and demethylated DNA, leading to interferon-alpha production and RNA binding autoantibodies such as antibodies against Ro La, Sm, and RNP. The TLR-independent pathway is activated by intracytoplasmic RNA sensors (RIG-1, MDA-5) and DNA sensors (IFI16, DAI) and leads to activation of IRF3 and NF-κB. Both self DNA/RNA and foreign DNA/RNA, such as from viruses, can lead to this activation. NETosis has recently gained attention in the pathogenesis of SLE. On activation by various factors such as cytokines, activated platelets, and vascular endothelial cells, neutrophils systematically release their nuclear aggregates in the extracellular environment. These nuclear aggregates can then promote Interferon-alpha production by the dendritic cells, mediate thrombosis and vascular damage and serve as self-antigens for T-lymphocytes.

retrieved_docs.title: Tumid Lupus Erythematosus -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Cutaneous lupus has a complex pathogenesis that relies on the interplay between genetic and environmental components. For all types of CCLE, including tumid lupus, UV radiation (specifically UVB) serves as a trigger. Immune alterations have also been implicated in the pathogenesis of TLE, specifically the decrease of T-regulatory cells and epidermal Langerhans cells and increased plasmacytoid dendritic cells. There is upregulation of type-1 interferon, tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-alpha), and Th17 cells. [12] It has been postulated that tobacco smoking is a risk factor for TLE. It has also been suggested that rare drug-induced forms of TLE exist in association with TNF-alpha inhibitors, thiazide diuretics, highly active antiretroviral therapy (HAART), angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors, bortezomib, and ustekinumab therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Acute Leukemia -- Interfering Factors -- Interfering Substances
retrieved_docs.content: Certain medications, such as heparin and specific antibiotics, may interfere with flow cytometry analysis by causing nonspecific binding or altering antigen expression. Additionally, high levels of paraproteins, as seen in conditions such as multiple myeloma, can disrupt immunophenotypic analysis.

retrieved_docs.title: Common Variable Immunodeficiency -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: B Cells abnormalities: The number of B cells is normal in the majority of patients, but many of them have reduced percentages of isotype-switched memory B cells capable of producing immunoglobin isotypes that are critical toward antibody response recall. The abnormalities that appear to occur in the bone marrow include aberrant gene rearrangement, decreased V gene replacements, reduced diversity of the naïve B cell repertoire, impaired somatic hypermutation, and abnormal expansion of unmutated B cell clones. [13] Toll-like receptor signaling: B cell maturation is defective, and the action of toll-like receptors 7 and 9 (TLR7 and TLR9) becomes impaired because of a yet unknown mechanism. Defective signaling of TLR7 and TLR9 in B cells and plasmacytoid dendritic cells, along with deficient IFN-a, impairs B cell functions and prevents TLR-mediated augmentation of humoral immunity in vivo. [14] [15] The binding of viral RNA to TLR7 or bacterial DNA to TLR9 leads to B cell activation, cytokine secretion, proliferation, and survival; in CVID, the loss of these stimuli functions may contribute to the B cell defects mentioned. [16] [17] Memory B cells: determining the number of isotype-switched memory B cells is of utmost interest because it provides information about the immaturity of the B cell, relates to the numbers of plasma cells in the bone marrow, and gives information about the possible clinical outcomes. [18] Various studies tried to classify CVID patients based on numbers of isotype-switched memory B cells [19] [20] [21] [22] [23] : Patients that switched CD27+IgM-IgD- memory B cells had a higher prevalence of splenomegaly and autoimmune cytopenias. This proposed a fast and reliable new classification for CVID patients with flow cytometric quantification of class-switched memory and immature B cells. Certain disorders were more prevalent in patients with the lowest count of memory B cells (splenomegaly, lymphoid proliferation, granulomatous disease). Lower levels of IgM memory B cells were associated with chronic pulmonary disease (bronchiectasis). Research has determined that the percentages of switched memory B cells changed in some patients over time. Based on these findings, the suggestion is that immunologic characteristics have to be evaluated several times after diagnosis. [24] Decreased T lymphocyte proliferation to mitogens and antigens, [1] reduced T regulatory cells, [25] T cell receptor excision circles that suggest thymic dysregulation, [26] defective T cell receptor signal transduction. Decrease in CD4+ cell count or increase in CD8+ cell count, altering the CD4/CD8 ratio. [27] Increase in CD26 and CD30 levels (the last ones associated with splenomegaly and malignancy) [28] . The minor capacity of dendritic cells to secrete interleukin-12. [29] A drop of plasma cells in the gastrointestinal tract and bone marrow. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Methotrexate -- Adverse Effects -- Drug-Drug Interactions
retrieved_docs.content: For patients with immune-mediated inflammatory diseases, methotrexate may adversely affect humoral and cellular immune responses to COVID-19 mRNA vaccines. While specific immunogenicity thresholds correlating with vaccine efficacy remain undefined, these findings suggest that alternative strategies and additional research may be needed. [30]

retrieved_docs.title: T-Cell Lymphoma -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Retinoids help through their antiproliferative and antiapoptotic properties. Commonly used topical regimens are bexarotene (FDA-approved) and tazarotene. Severe hyperlipidemia and central hypothyroidism are side effects of bexarotene. Systemic retinoids include acitretin, isotretinoin, and bexarotene. Topical chemotherapy commonly used are mechlorethamine (nitrogen mustard), an alkylating agent, and carmustine. Systemic chemotherapy commonly used is methotrexate, pralatrexate, chlorambucil, gemcitabine, pegylated doxorubicin, and antimetabolite like fludarabine phosphate, 2-deoxycoformycin. PUVA inhibits DNA and RNA synthesis, but it will affect both normal and neoplastic cells; thus, the side effect profile is secondary malignancies of the skin. Histone deacetylase inhibitors (HDACi) are the newer group of therapeutic options available for the treatment of CTCL, which have both transcription-dependent and independent mechanisms of action for the expression of tumor suppressor genes. Vorinostat and romidepsin are FDA-approved, with an overall response rate of almost 50% with a tolerable side effect profile, the prominent one being cardiac arrhythmias from QTc prolongation. Targeted therapies include imiquimod (Toll-like receptor agonist), denileukin diftitox (recombinant fusion protein), alemtuzumab (a monoclonal antibody against CD52), and everolimus ( mTOR pathway target). Agents that are still undertrial are proteasome inhibitors like bortezomib. Allogenic stem cell transplant is used in advanced stages, usually appropriate for young patients with relapsing disease. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 798
retrieved_docs.content: BCG vaccine, aminopterin, azacytidine, busulfan, floxuridine, fluorouracil, leukovorin, melphalan, mitobronitol, nafoxidine, and poly A‑U are all classified as antitumor substances. These agents represent a diverse group of chemotherapeutic and immunotherapeutic compounds used in cancer treatment. BCG vaccine functions as an immunotherapy that stimulates the immune system against tumor cells. Aminopterin is an antifolate that interferes with DNA synthesis. Azacytidine is a nucleoside analog that incorporates into RNA and DNA, leading to hypomethylation and cytotoxicity. Busulfan is an alkylating agent that cross‑links DNA strands. Floxuridine and fluorouracil are pyrimidine analogs that inhibit thymidylate synthase, disrupting DNA synthesis. Leukovorin (folinic acid) is often used to modulate the toxicity of fluoropyrimidines and enhance their efficacy. Melphalan is another alkylating agent used in multiple myeloma. Mitobronitol is a nitrosourea derivative with alkylating activity. Nafoxidine is a selective estrogen‑receptor modulator with antineoplastic properties. Poly A‑U, a synthetic double‑stranded RNA, has been investigated for its ability to induce interferon production and antitumor effects. Collectively, these agents belong to the broader pharmacological category of nafazetrom, indicating that they share a common therapeutic purpose of targeting malignant cells. Understanding the relationships among these antitumor substances helps clinicians select appropriate regimens and anticipate synergistic or antagonistic interactions in oncology care.

retrieved_docs.content: mitoguazon, mitotan, arsenic trioxide and pentostatin. Anagrelide’s antineoplastic effect is mediated by phosphodiesterase inhibition, illustrating enzyme‑targeted mechanisms within this class. The FOLFIRI chemotherapy regimen incorporates irinotecan, a topoisomerase I inhibitor, while methotrexate sodium acts as an antifolate that interferes with DNA synthesis. Talimogene laherparepvec represents an oncolytic viral therapy derived from human herpesvirus 1, highlighting virotherapy as a distinct antitumor strategy. Collectively, these relationships delineate a comprehensive framework in which antitumorózní látky serve as the overarching therapeutic class, encompassing diverse chemical, biological and viral agents with varied mechanisms—ranging from cytotoxicity, enzyme inhibition, corticosteroid‑mediated support to immune‑modulating virotherapy—used for the treatment of hematologic malignancies such as leukemia and other cancers, while also identifying specific contraindications and

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 1 -- Community 110
retrieved_docs.content: **estramustine**, **nordihydroguaiaretic acid**, **mitoguazone**, and **hexamethylmelamine**. These antineoplastic substances, including **cortisone acetate**, **cytarabine**, **dex**amethasone acetate, **dex**amethasone phosphate, **dex**amethasonisonikotinát, **irinotecan**, **irinotecan hydrochloride**, **methotrexate sodium**, **pipobroman**, **prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate**, **prednisolone sodium phosphate**, and **prednisolone tebutate**, are indicated for the treatment of **leukemia** (Arabic: ابْيضَاضُ الدَّم), a disease classified within the broader group of leukemias. Their use is contraindicated in patients with an active infection of **human herpesvirus 1 (HHV‑1)** and in those receiving the oncolytic viral therapy **talimogene laherparepvec**, due to the risk of adverse drug‑virus interactions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 275
retrieved_docs.content: The ATC class “other antineoplastic agents” comprises a heterogeneous group of chemotherapeutic and immunotherapeutic substances that are also frequently classified as antagonists or inhibitors because they oppose or suppress specific biological pathways. Within this class, agents such as BCG vaccine, aminopterin, amitriptyline, asparaginase, azacitidine (azacytidine), busulfan, cyclophosphamide, cytarabine, dactinomycin, daunorubicin (daunomycin), estramustine, floxuridine, fluorouracil, guanfacine, hexamethylmelamine, hydroxymethane (hydroxyurea), nordihydroguaiaretic acid, kaolin, nadolol, leucovorin, melphalan, mitobronitol, mitoguazon, mitotan, nafoxidine, oxid arsenitý (arsenic trioxide), pentostatin, poly A‑U, and nafazatrom are listed as antagonists/inhibitors. These agents exert therapeutic effects by inhibiting enzymes, DNA synthesis, folate metabolism, or other oncogenic pathways, a property that influences their clinical use, potential drug interactions, and side‑effect profiles. Several drugs—dactinomycin, daunorubicin, and the BCG vaccine—are identified as “child agents” of the broader antitumor substance group, highlighting their specific role in cancer treatment. Asparaginase belongs to the enzyme class hydrolases, reflecting its mechanism of hydrolyzing asparagine, while nafazatrom is explicitly linked to the antineoplastic class through an “isa” relationship. The inclusion of oxid arsenitý and pentostatin together illustrates the grouping of miscellaneous anticancer agents within the same ATC category. Overall, the hierarchical and membership relationships among these compounds clarify their classification, mechanisms of action, and clinical applications in oncology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 539
retrieved_docs.content: BCG vakcína and aminopterin are classified as antitumorózní látky (antitumor substances), a broad group that also includes azacytidin, busulfan, floxuridin, fluorouracil, leukovorin, mitobronitol, nafoxidin and poly A‑U. These agents exemplify the diverse mechanisms used in cancer therapy. BCG vakcína acts as an immunotherapy that stimulates the host immune response against tumor cells, whereas aminopterin is a folate antagonist that blocks DNA synthesis. Azacytidin is a nucleoside analog that incorporates into RNA and DNA, causing hypomethylation and cytotoxicity. Busulfan is an alkylating agent that cross‑links DNA strands, leading to cell death. Floxuridin and fluorouracil are pyrimidine analogs that inhibit thymidylate synthase, disrupting DNA replication. Leukovorin (folinic acid) is frequently used to modulate the toxicity of fluoropyrimidines and to enhance their antitumor efficacy. Mitobronitol, a nitrosourea derivative, alkylates DNA, and nafoxidin, a selective estrogen‑receptor modulator, exerts antineoplastic effects. Poly A‑U, a synthetic double‑stranded RNA, can induce interferon production and display antitumor activity. Together, these antitumorózní látky illustrate a spectrum of therapeutic strategies—including immune activation, enzyme inhibition, DNA alkylation, and nucleic‑acid analog incorporation—highlighting the importance of tailored agent selection for effective patient care in oncology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 1 -- Community 53
retrieved_docs.content: are also linked to the antitumor category; human herpesvirus 1 functions as a vector in oncolytic virotherapy and talimogene laherparepvec, an engineered oncolytic herpesvirus approved for melanoma, is contraindicated in patients with leukemia, underscoring specific safety considerations. Collectively, these relationships demonstrate that antitumor agents span traditional cytotoxic drugs, corticosteroids, targeted inhibitors, and emerging viral therapies, reflecting the multifaceted strategies employed in contemporary cancer treatment and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 84

Collectively, these agents act through diverse mechanisms—including DNA‑synthesis inhibition, alkylation, antimetabolite effects, microtubule disruption, phosphodiesterase inhibition, and immune‑mediated viral oncolysis—allowing clinicians to select appropriate regimens for cancer treatment and supportive care while observing specific contraindications such as HHV‑1 infection

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 908
retrieved_docs.content: BCG vaccine and aminopterin are identified as specific antitumor agents within the broader class of antitumor substances. The antitumor substance category also encompasses a range of chemotherapeutic compounds, including azacytidine, busulfan, floxuridine, fluorouracil, leukovorin, mitobronitol, nafoxidine, and the nucleic‑acid analogue poly A‑U. These agents are collectively used in oncology to interfere with DNA synthesis, alkylate nucleic acids, or modulate folate pathways, thereby inhibiting tumor growth. In addition to their oncologic applications, antitumor substances are linked therapeutically to human herpesvirus 1, reflecting their potential antiviral activity or use in managing virus‑associated malignancies. Prednisolone acetate, a corticosteroid, is also associated with the antitumor substance group, indicating its occasional role as an adjunctive anti‑inflammatory or immunosuppressive component in cancer treatment protocols. Together, these relationships illustrate the hierarchical classification of antitumor agents, their diverse pharmacologic members, and their relevance to both cancer therapy and related viral conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1385
retrieved_docs.content: Toxicological considerations also extend to the development, evaluation, and administration of viral vaccines, requiring vigilance for any toxic risks associated with vaccine components or adjuvants. Comprehensive awareness of the toxic profiles of these agents, together with systematic application of the Allowed qualifier, is essential for optimizing patient care and ensuring the safety of therapeutic and preventive interventions.

This mapping highlights the structural similarity of abacavir to dideoxyadenosine, which can be relevant for understanding its mechanism of action, potential cross‑reactivity, and the design of related therapeutic agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 479
retrieved_docs.content: BCG vaccine, aminopterin, azacytidine, busulfan, cytarabine, floxuridine, fluorouracil, leucovorin, mitobronitol, nafoxidine, and poly A‑U are classified as antitumor agents. These compounds represent a diverse group of chemotherapeutic and immunotherapeutic substances used in cancer treatment. BCG vaccine is an immunotherapy that stimulates the immune system to attack tumor cells, particularly in bladder cancer. Aminopterin is a folate antagonist that inhibits DNA synthesis. Azacytidine is a nucleoside analog that incorporates into RNA and DNA, leading to hypomethylation and cytotoxicity. Busulfan is an alkylating agent that cross‑links DNA strands. Cytarabine is a pyrimidine analog that interferes with DNA polymerase. Floxuridine and fluorouracil are fluoropyrimidine antimetabolites that disrupt thymidylate synthesis. Leucovorin, although primarily a folinic‑acid rescue agent, is used in combination with fluoropyrimidines to enhance their cytotoxic effect. Mitobronitol is a nitrosourea derivative that alkylates DNA. Nafoxidine is a selective estrogen‑receptor modulator with antineoplastic activity in hormone‑dependent cancers. Poly A‑U, a synthetic double‑stranded RNA, can induce interferon production and exhibit antitumor activity. Together, these agents illustrate the range of mechanisms—immune activation, DNA synthesis inhibition, alkylation, antimetabolite interference, hormonal modulation, and interferon induction—employed in modern oncology to target malignant cells.
